exemplar.title: How has the amount of rainfall changed over the years in Spain, and how does this trend compare to rainfall patterns in the Netherlands?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The report follows a recognisable sequence with a clear introduction, body and conclusion.
Most material is directly relevant to the research question, demonstrating focused content.
Headings and basic structure allow the reader to follow the overall argument.
weaknesses: Repetition of earlier points (e.g. standardâdeviation discussion appears twice) interrupts the narrative flow.
Transitions between sections are abrupt and some logical links (e.g. choice of deciles after box-plots) are only partially explained.
Headings are uneven (no separate methodology section, inconsistent figure/table captions) and some content could be more concise.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments consistently place this work in band 2: coherent in places but interrupted by redundancy, structure that is adequate yet incomplete, and mostly relevant content with some unnecessary elements. These features justify a Presentation score of 2.

strengths: Variables are named and units are given, and symbols such as Î²â and Î²â appear throughout.
A variety of representations (box-plots, bar charts, scatter-plots, regression tables) support the analysis.
Paragraphs generally begin with a claim followed by justification, showing an attempt at logical communication.
weaknesses: Notation is sometimes incorrect or inconsistent (e.g. variance in mmÂ² vs mm, misformatted râ).
Axes and units are frequently missing or unclear, and confidence bands are not explained.
Several arguments contain minor logical gaps or circular reasoning, limiting overall clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands were judged at band 2: some appropriate mathematical language and notation, some use of different representations, and a moderate level of logical clarity. This supports a Mathematical Communication score of 2.

strengths: The studentâs personal interest (motivation by travel and environmental concern) is clearly communicated.
Concrete extension ideas (adding predictors, exploring non-linear models) demonstrate planning for further work.
weaknesses: Evidence of independent thinking is minimal, with most techniques following standard procedures.
The mathematical approach lacks innovation beyond the prescribed statistical template.
Creative or personal adaptations of methodology are limited.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand judgements place Personal Engagement firmly in band 1: there is a personal voice and some initiative, but only limited independent thought and creativity. Hence a score of 1 is appropriate.

strengths: A short reflection section identifies data-size problems and suggests more predictors.
Basic commentary on p-values and RÂ² shows an attempt to interpret outcomes.
weaknesses: Reflection remains surface-level with no deep connection to broader implications or method assumptions.
Analysis of results is basic, lacking critique of model assumptions or data reliability.
Evaluation of outcomes does not quantify error or discuss practical significance.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All reflection strands were assessed at band 1: limited reflection, basic critical analysis, and minimal evaluation of outcomes. This justifies a Reflection score of 1.

strengths: Descriptive statistics, correlation coefficients and OLS regression are all directly relevant and applied appropriately.
Techniques used are commensurate with the SL syllabus and correctly contextualized for the data sets.
Calculations and interpretations demonstrate a solid, outline understanding of statistical methods.
weaknesses: Conceptual errors appear (misinterpretation of variance units, miscalculated standard deviations).
The logic of p-value interpretation is sometimes reversed or incomplete.
Analysis stops at single-variable linear models, with no advanced or alternative modeling to deepen the investigation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Relevance and level appropriateness meet band 3 criteria, and understandingâthough marred by some errorsâstill demonstrates a working competence. These factors support a Use of Mathematics score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Paragraphs usually begin with a declarative sentence followed by justification, but several arguments are circular (e.g. citing low RÂ² then still claiming âslight increaseâ). Minor logical gaps remain, yet the discussion is not entirely disorganised. This places the work in the middle band for clarity.

The student demonstrates an outline understanding of each statistical measure, yet conceptual errors appear (misinterpreting variance units, small-sample regression justified despite acknowledged non-normality). Calculations appear plausible but are not verified; hence understanding is partial with some inaccuracies, matching band 2.

The student provides box-plots, bar charts, scatter-plots, regression tables and descriptive statistics, demonstrating more than one form of representation. However, axes are occasionally missing units, confidence bands are unexplained, and the frequency charts use unclear binning. Hence the evidence best fits âsome useâ rather than âeffective use.â

The report follows a recognisable sequence (introduction â data description â basic statistics â correlation/regression â conclusion) so the reader can follow the argument, yet the narrative is frequently interrupted by repetition of earlier points (e.g. the standard-deviation discussion appears twice) and several transitions are abrupt. Logical links between sections (for example, why deciles were chosen after box-plots) are only partially explained. These features match âsome coherenceâ rather than the smoother flow required for the higher bands.

The investigation employs common descriptive statistics and simple regression; there is no attempt to experiment with alternative models beyond a passing suggestion in the reflection. Creativity and initiative are therefore limited.

The student reports p-values and RÂ² but offers only elementary commentary (e.g. âno relationshipâ). There is no deeper critique of model assumptions or data reliability; thus the analysis is basic rather than thoughtful.

While the student states that the null hypothesis is not rejected, there is no quantitative assessment of error size or practical significance, nor any discussion of how conclusions might influence further study. This represents âlimited evaluation of outcomes.â

Reflection is confined to one short section listing data-size problems and suggesting more predictors. It remains surface-level and does not connect findings to broader climate implications or to the mathematical methods used. This aligns with âlimited reflectionâ.

Variables are named, units given, and symbols such as Î²â, Î²â are used, yet notation is sometimes incorrect (variance quoted in mmÂ² but later compared directly with mm; ârsâ typed as r s). Explanations of r and p-value are worded informally. This mixture aligns with âsome appropriate mathematical language.â

The personal voice appears mainly in the introduction and brief reflections, but the mathematical work follows a standard statistical template. Consequently, evidence of a personal approach is present but minimal, corresponding to band 1.

Most material is relevant to the research question, but long descriptive paragraphs restate statistics already shown in tables and the discussion of setting a â20 % highâlow rangeâ adds little mathematical value. The length could be trimmed substantially; hence it is âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elements.â

Means, medians, variance, standard deviation, correlation coefficients and OLS regression are all directly relevant to analysing rainfall trends. The mathematics is applied appropriately to both data sets, satisfying the descriptor for band 3. However, the work stops at single-variable linear models, so it is not âcomprehensiveâ (band 5) or âexpertâ (band 6).

Apart from choosing rainfall because the student ânoticed Spain was hotterâ, there is little sign of generating or adapting mathematics independently. Most techniques replicate textbook procedures without extension or novel insight, indicating only limited independent thinking.

There is a clear introduction, body and conclusion, but headings are uneven (no separate methodology section, tables sometimes embedded without captions). Figures are referenced, yet numbering resets and some labels appear after the graphic. Such basic but incomplete structure fits band 2, whereas band 3 requires a consistently clear structure.

All techniques employedâsummary statistics, Pearson/Spearman correlation, linear regressionâare within the AA SL syllabus and used in a contextually appropriate way. No advanced or HL-level material is attempted, so âfully commensurateâ (band 4) is arguable but the minor misapplications lower confidence, placing it safely in band 3.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated and focused, but could be refined by specifying the statistical methods or time intervals to be analyzed to strengthen the investigationâs scope.
The key concept 'Modelling waterfall, Nature, and natural resources' is vague and not integrated into the analysis. Clarify how this concept informs the mathematical exploration.
The studentâs personal interest is effectively communicated, demonstrating genuine motivation through travel experience and environmental concern.
This paragraph largely restates the research question and context. Consider consolidating or eliminating redundancy to improve conciseness.
The studyâs aim is clear but wordy. Try to express the objective more concisely and directly link it to the chosen mathematical methods.
The box plot caption states mean and median but the graphic displays only the median. Indicate clearly whether mean was calculated separately and differentiate it from the box plot median.
Terminology like 'max range' and 'minimum range' is nonstandard. Replace with 'upper whisker' and 'lower whisker' or specify outlier boundaries.
In the Netherlands box plot, the median and mean values conflict (848 mm vs 800 mm). Ensure consistent use of summary statistics and verify values.
When comparing Spain and the Netherlands, explicitly reference the figuresâ labels and clarify which statistic (mean or median) is being compared for clarity.
The narrative incorrectly references a variance squared within a range discussion. Clarify the units: variance is in mmÂ² and cannot be directly compared to mm without taking a square root.
Calculation of high and low standard deviations uses values 36.7 mm and 24.5 mm, but earlier stated standard deviation is 31.7 mm. Double-check your multiplication by 1Â±0.20.
The high standard deviation for the Netherlands is miscalculated as 36.7 mm, but 23.0Ã1.2=27.6 mm. Recompute these values accurately.
The concluding comparison repeats earlier content almost verbatim. Consider merging this with the Spain sectionâs final sentence to avoid unnecessary repetition.
Axes and units are missing for the frequency tables. Include labels for the horizontal (deciles) and vertical (frequency) axes to improve readability.
The interpretation of decile frequencies is unclear (e.g., '70% lower frequency'). Clarify which deciles correspond to high/low frequencies and provide actual counts or percentages.
Explain why both Pearson and Spearman tests are applied, and specify their suitability given potential non-normal distribution of the data.
The heading 'Coefficient correlation Test' is not formatted consistently with section titles. Use proper heading styles to enhance structure.
Include the p-values alongside correlation coefficients (r and râ) when interpreting significance to support claims about relationship strength.
The notation 'rs = 0.313' should be formatted as $$r_s = 0.313$$ for consistency with statistical conventions.
The comparison 'no linear nor monotonic relationship' lacks p-value context. Report the associated p-values to justify conclusions.
The regression model is presented correctly, but the student should discuss underlying assumptions (linearity, independence, homoscedasticity) to critically assess model validity.
Hypotheses are not formatted in standard mathematical notation. Use $$H_0:$$ and $$H_a:$$ statements with inline math for clarity and correctness.
The regression output figure lacks a caption and axis labels. Number and caption each figure and ensure that all table headers and column titles are legible.
When describing the line of best fitâs slope, include the estimated coefficient value and its unit interpretation (e.g., mm per year) to strengthen the analysis.
Describing the slope as 'straight' is imprecise. Quantify the slope magnitude and discuss if the change is statistically and practically significant.
The regression table is dense and includes many diagnostics. Highlight only the key parameters (Î²â, Î²â, p-values, RÂ²) in the main text, moving less relevant diagnostics to an appendix.
The logic regarding p-value interpretation is reversed: a p-value <0.05 indicates a significant relationship leading to rejecting Hâ, not 'no significant relationship.' Correct this misconception.
In the Spain regression table, merge empty columns or remove unused cells to improve readability, and align table layout with the Netherlands table.
In the limitations and reflection, discuss how the non-normal distribution and small sample size specifically affect parameter estimates and p-value reliability.
The conclusion summarises methods but lacks synthesis of findings. Connect results back to the research question and draw clear conclusions supported by the data.
The student proposes concrete extensions such as adding predictors and exploring non-linear models, showing initiative and planning for further investigation.
